expanded_description:
The Phillips curve is not stable; it shifts over time due to changes in inflation expectations, supply shocks, and changes in the natural rate of unemployment. Understanding these shifts is essential for interpreting the relationship between inflation and unemployment, for forecasting inflation, and for designing monetary policy. A shift in the Phillips curve means that at the same level of unemployment, inflation is different—either higher (a bad shift) or lower (a good shift).

There are three main sources of shifts in the Phillips curve: (1) changes in expected inflation ($\pi^e$), which shift the short-run Phillips curve up or down; (2) supply shocks ($\nu$), such as oil price changes or productivity changes, which also shift the SRPC; and (3) changes in the natural rate of unemployment ($u_n$), which shift the long-run Phillips curve and also affect the SRPC. The 1970s oil crises caused stagflation (high inflation and high unemployment) by shifting the SRPC up. The 1990s productivity boom shifted the SRPC down, allowing low unemployment without inflation. The COVID-19 pandemic caused both supply shocks (shifting the SRPC up) and changes in the natural rate (as workers left the labor force).

In this section, we will examine the factors that shift the Phillips curve, analyze the effects of supply shocks (both adverse and favorable), discuss changes in the natural rate and their causes, explore historical episodes (1970s oil shocks, 1990s productivity boom, 2008 financial crisis, COVID-19 pandemic), and evaluate the policy implications for central banks. You will learn to distinguish between movements along the Phillips curve (changes in unemployment) and shifts of the Phillips curve (changes in expectations, supply, or natural rate), and to analyze how supply shocks affect the trade-off facing policymakers.

core_concept.principle:
The Phillips curve shifts due to three main factors: changes in expected inflation ($\pi^e$), supply shocks ($ u$), and changes in the natural rate of unemployment ($u_n$). The general form of the expectations-augmented Phillips curve is:

$\pi = \pi^e - eta (u - u_n) + u$

where:
• $\pi^e$ = expected inflation
• $ eta$ = slope parameter (sensitivity of inflation to cyclical unemployment)
• $u_n$ = natural rate of unemployment (NAIRU)
• $ u$ = supply shock (positive $ u$ = adverse shock, increases inflation)

Sources of Shifts:

1. Changes in Expected Inflation ($\pi^e$):
• Higher $\pi^e$ shifts the SRPC up (higher inflation at each unemployment rate).
• Lower $\pi^e$ shifts the SRPC down.
• Expectations can change due to central bank credibility, policy announcements, or past inflation.
• Example: The Volcker disinflation lowered $\pi^e$ over time, shifting the SRPC down.

2. Supply Shocks ($ u$):
• Adverse supply shock ($ u > 0$): oil price increase, supply chain disruption, pandemic. Shifts SRPC up (higher inflation at each $u$). Causes stagflation (high inflation + high unemployment).
• Favorable supply shock ($ u < 0$): oil price decrease, productivity boom, technological improvement. Shifts SRPC down (lower inflation at each $u$). Allows low unemployment with low inflation.
• Examples: 1973 oil crisis ($ u > 0$), 1990s IT productivity boom ($ u < 0$), COVID-19 supply chain disruptions ($ u > 0$), 2023-2024 supply chain normalization ($ u < 0$).

3. Changes in the Natural Rate ($u_n$):
• Increase in $u_n$ shifts the LRPC right and also shifts the SRPC (the point where $\pi = \pi^e$ moves).
• Decrease in $u_n$ shifts the LRPC left.
• Causes of changes in $u_n$: demographics, labor market institutions, technology, globalization, hysteresis.
• Example: 1990s decline in $u_n$ (from 6% to 4%) allowed lower unemployment without inflation.

Distinguishing Movements vs. Shifts:
• Movement along the SRPC: Change in $u$ caused by monetary policy, with $\pi^e$, $u_n$, and $ u$ constant.
• Shift of the SRPC: Change in $\pi^e$, $ u$, or $u_n$.

Historical Episodes:
• 1970s oil shocks: $ u > 0$ (adverse supply shocks) shifted SRPC up; stagflation.
• 1980s Volcker disinflation: Movement along SRPC (unemployment up) then SRPC shifted down as $\pi^e$ fell.
• 1990s productivity boom: $ u < 0$ (favorable supply shock) shifted SRPC down; low unemployment without inflation.
• 2008 financial crisis: Increase in $u_n$ (due to hysteresis) shifted LRPC right; also demand shock.
• COVID-19 pandemic (2020-2022): $ u > 0$ (supply chain disruptions, labor shortages) shifted SRPC up; also fiscal stimulus increased demand; inflation surged.
• 2023-2024 supply chain normalization: $ u < 0$ (favorable supply shock) shifted SRPC down, helping disinflation without deep recession.
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine the Phillips curve as a line on a graph with inflation on the vertical axis and unemployment on the horizontal axis. A movement along the curve occurs when the central bank changes aggregate demand: lower unemployment (tight labor market) leads to higher inflation; higher unemployment leads to lower inflation.

Now imagine the entire curve shifting up. At the same unemployment rate, inflation is higher. This is what happened in the 1970s: oil prices quadrupled, shifting the SRPC up. The economy experienced stagflation: both inflation and unemployment were high.

Now imagine the curve shifting down. At the same unemployment rate, inflation is lower. This happened in the 1990s: the IT productivity boom shifted the SRPC down, allowing the US to have unemployment as low as 4% with inflation only 2%.

Finally, imagine the vertical long-run Phillips curve shifting right. This means the natural rate of unemployment has increased. This happened in Europe in the 1980s and after the 2008 crisis in the US (temporarily).

Understanding these shifts is crucial for central banks. If the SRPC shifts up (supply shock), the central bank faces a worse trade-off: reducing inflation requires a larger increase in unemployment. The central bank must decide whether to 'accommodate' the shock (allow higher inflation) or 'fight' it (cause a recession). The 1970s Fed accommodated initially, leading to entrenched inflation. The 2022 Fed fought inflation aggressively, causing a slowdown but (so far) avoiding a deep recession.

image.alt: Three graphs: SRPC shifting up (adverse supply shock, stagflation), SRPC shifting down (favorable supply shock, low inflation at low unemployment), LRPC shifting right (increase in natural rate).
image.caption: Figure 10.4.1: The Phillips curve can shift due to changes in expected inflation, supply shocks, or changes in the natural rate of unemployment.

sections.introduction:
The Phillips curve is not stable; it shifts over time due to changes in inflation expectations, supply shocks, and changes in the natural rate of unemployment. Understanding these shifts is essential for interpreting the relationship between inflation and unemployment, for forecasting inflation, and for designing monetary policy. A shift in the Phillips curve means that at the same level of unemployment, inflation is different—either higher (a bad shift) or lower (a good shift).

There are three main sources of shifts in the Phillips curve: (1) changes in expected inflation ($\pi^e$), which shift the short-run Phillips curve up or down; (2) supply shocks ($\nu$), such as oil price changes or productivity changes, which also shift the SRPC; and (3) changes in the natural rate of unemployment ($u_n$), which shift the long-run Phillips curve and also affect the SRPC. The 1970s oil crises caused stagflation (high inflation and high unemployment) by shifting the SRPC up. The 1990s productivity boom shifted the SRPC down, allowing low unemployment without inflation. The COVID-19 pandemic caused both supply shocks (shifting the SRPC up) and changes in the natural rate (as workers left the labor force).

In this section, we will examine the factors that shift the Phillips curve, analyze the effects of supply shocks (both adverse and favorable), discuss changes in the natural rate and their causes, explore historical episodes, and evaluate the policy implications for central banks.

introduction_image.alt: Timeline: 1970s oil shocks (SRPC up), 1980s Volcker disinflation (movement then shift down), 1990s productivity boom (SRPC down), 2008 crisis (natural rate up), 2020 pandemic (SRPC up), 2023 normalization (SRPC down).
introduction_image.caption: Figure 10.4.2: Historical shifts in the Phillips curve: supply shocks, changes in expectations, and changes in the natural rate have caused the curve to move over time.

key_concept.definition:
Shifts in the Phillips Curve: Changes in the relationship between inflation and unemployment caused by factors other than cyclical unemployment.

The General Phillips Curve Equation:
$\pi = \pi^e - eta (u - u_n) + u$

Three Sources of Shifts:

1. Changes in Expected Inflation ($\pi^e$):
• Higher $\pi^e$ → SRPC shifts up (higher inflation at each $u$).
• Lower $\pi^e$ → SRPC shifts down.
• Expectations are influenced by central bank credibility, past inflation, and policy announcements.
• Example: The Volcker disinflation (1980s) lowered $\pi^e$ over time, shifting the SRPC down.

2. Supply Shocks ($ u$):
• $ u > 0$ (adverse shock): oil price increase, supply chain disruption, natural disaster, pandemic. Shifts SRPC up. Causes stagflation (high inflation + high unemployment).
• $ u < 0$ (favorable shock): oil price decrease, productivity boom, technological innovation. Shifts SRPC down. Allows low unemployment with low inflation.
• Examples: 1973 oil crisis ($ u > 0$), 1990s IT productivity boom ($ u < 0$), COVID-19 supply disruptions ($ u > 0$).

3. Changes in the Natural Rate ($u_n$):
• $u_n \uparrow$ (higher NAIRU): LRPC shifts right; SRPC also shifts (the point where $\pi = \pi^e$ moves to higher $u$).
• $u_n \downarrow$ (lower NAIRU): LRPC shifts left.
• Causes: demographics (aging workforce), labor market institutions (unemployment insurance, minimum wage), technology (skill mismatch), globalization, hysteresis (recessions increase $u_n$).
• Example: 1990s decline in US $u_n$ from 6% to 4% (aging workforce, increased incarceration, improved labor market matching).

Distinguishing Movements vs. Shifts:
• Movement along SRPC: Change in $u$ due to monetary policy (demand shock), with $\pi^e$, $u_n$, $ u$ constant.
• Shift of SRPC: Change in $\pi^e$, $ u$, or $u_n$.

Policy Implications:
• Supply shocks create a dilemma: fighting inflation requires raising unemployment (recession); accommodating inflation allows higher inflation to persist.
• Credible central banks can offset supply shocks by anchoring expectations (preventing $\pi^e$ from rising).
• Changes in the natural rate require structural policies (education, training, labor market reform), not monetary policy.
• Central banks must distinguish between cyclical and structural changes in unemployment to set policy correctly.
key_concept.context: The stability of the Phillips curve has been debated since the 1970s. The original Phillips curve (no expectations) appeared stable in the 1950s-1960s but broke down in the 1970s. The expectations-augmented Phillips curve (Friedman-Phelps) explained shifts due to changing expectations. Supply shocks (oil prices) added another source of shifts. In the 1990s, the Phillips curve shifted down (favorable supply shocks from IT productivity). In the 2008 crisis, the natural rate increased temporarily (hysteresis). In the 2020s, the pandemic caused both supply shocks (shifting the SRPC up) and changes in the natural rate (labor force exits). Understanding these shifts is crucial for inflation forecasting and policy design.

image.alt: Diagram: Three arrows pointing to Phillips curve: π^e (up/down), ν (supply shock), un (natural rate shift).
image.caption: Figure 10.4.3: Three factors shift the Phillips curve: changes in expected inflation, supply shocks, and changes in the natural rate of unemployment.

classifications.value: Adverse supply shocks shift the SRPC up, causing stagflation; favorable supply shocks shift it down, allowing low unemployment with low inflation
characteristics: Adverse supply shock ($\nu > 0$): oil price increase, supply chain disruption, pandemic, natural disaster.
Effect: SRPC shifts up → higher inflation at every unemployment rate.
Causes stagflation: high inflation + high unemployment (worse trade-off).
Central bank faces dilemma: accommodate (let inflation rise) or fight (cause recession).
Favorable supply shock ($\nu < 0$): productivity boom, oil price decrease, technological innovation.
Effect: SRPC shifts down → lower inflation at every unemployment rate.
Allows 'immaculate disinflation' (low unemployment with low inflation).
classifications.behavior:
Supply shocks are events that directly affect production costs, shifting the aggregate supply curve. In the Phillips curve framework, they shift the SRPC up (adverse) or down (favorable).

Adverse Supply Shock (1970s oil crisis): Oil prices quadrupled, increasing production costs. The SRPC shifted up. At the same unemployment rate, inflation was higher. The economy experienced stagflation: high inflation (10%+) and high unemployment (9%). The central bank faced a dilemma: if it tightened to fight inflation, unemployment would rise further; if it accommodated, inflation would become entrenched. The Fed under Volcker chose to fight, causing a deep recession but eventually bringing inflation down.

Favorable Supply Shock (1990s IT boom): The information technology revolution increased productivity, reducing production costs. The SRPC shifted down. The US experienced low unemployment (4%) with low inflation (2%). This was a 'free lunch'—the Fed could keep rates low without worrying about inflation. The natural rate also fell during this period.

COVID-19 Pandemic (2020-2022): Supply chain disruptions, labor shortages, and energy price spikes created adverse supply shocks, shifting the SRPC up. Combined with massive fiscal stimulus (demand shock), inflation surged to 9%. The Fed tightened aggressively, causing a slowdown but (so far) avoiding a deep recession. By 2023-2024, supply chain normalization created favorable supply shocks, shifting the SRPC down and helping disinflation.

image.alt: Graph: SRPC shifting up (adverse shock) → stagflation (higher π and u). SRPC shifting down (favorable shock) → lower π at same u.
image.caption: Figure 10.4.4: Adverse supply shocks shift the SRPC up, causing stagflation. Favorable supply shocks shift it down, allowing low unemployment with low inflation.

examples.explanation: OPEC raised oil prices from \$3 to \$12 per barrel (400% increase). The SRPC shifted up. Inflation rose from 3% to 11%, and unemployment rose from 4% to 9%. The US experienced stagflation. The Fed initially accommodated, but Volcker later tightened, causing a recession.

The IT revolution increased productivity growth from 1.5% to 3% per year. The SRPC shifted down. Unemployment fell to 4% while inflation remained at 2%. This favorable supply shock allowed the Fed to keep rates low without causing inflation.

Pandemic caused factory closures, port delays, and labor shortages. The SRPC shifted up. Combined with fiscal stimulus, inflation rose to 9%. The Fed raised rates aggressively. By 2023-2024, supply chains normalized, creating a favorable supply shock that helped disinflation.

classifications.value: Changes in expected inflation shift the SRPC; credible central banks can anchor expectations to reduce shifts
characteristics: $\pi^e \uparrow$ → SRPC shifts up (higher inflation at each $u$).
Expectations are influenced by past inflation, central bank credibility, and policy announcements.
Anchored expectations: $\pi^e$ stable around target, even when actual inflation fluctuates.
Unanchored expectations: $\pi^e$ moves with actual inflation (adaptive expectations).
Credible central banks can shift the SRPC down without a recession (by lowering $\pi^e$).
classifications.behavior:
Expected inflation is a key determinant of the SRPC's position. When people expect higher inflation, they demand higher wages and set higher prices, shifting the SRPC up. When they expect lower inflation, the SRPC shifts down.

The Volcker disinflation (1979-1982) demonstrated the importance of expectations. The Fed raised rates, causing a deep recession. Over time, as the public observed lower inflation, expected inflation fell, shifting the SRPC down. The sacrifice ratio was high because expectations adjusted slowly (adaptive expectations).

In contrast, by the 1990s, the Fed had built credibility. When the Fed signaled a tightening, expected inflation did not rise (or even fell). The SRPC remained low, allowing a 'soft landing' (1994-1995).

Inflation targeting (adopted by many central banks in the 1990s) is designed to anchor expectations. By announcing a numerical target (e.g., 2%) and being held accountable, central banks prevent expectations from becoming unanchored. Even when actual inflation deviates (e.g., 2021-2022), anchored expectations keep the SRPC from shifting up as much as it would have in the 1970s.

Forward guidance is another tool for influencing expectations directly. The central bank announces its future policy path, shaping expectations of future inflation and interest rates.

image.alt: Graph: SRPC shifting up when π^e rises; shifting down when π^e falls. Anchored expectations keep SRPC stable.
image.caption: Figure 10.4.5: Changes in expected inflation shift the SRPC. Credible central banks can anchor expectations, keeping the SRPC stable.

examples.explanation: In 1979, expected inflation was 8-9%. As the Fed tightened, actual inflation fell, but expected inflation fell slowly (adaptive expectations). The SRPC shifted down gradually, requiring a prolonged recession. The sacrifice ratio was high.
By 1994, the Fed had credibility. When the Fed raised rates preemptively, expected inflation remained stable around 2-3%. The SRPC did not shift up. The Fed achieved a soft landing (no recession).

Despite actual inflation reaching 9%, long-term inflation expectations remained anchored around 2-2.5% (TIPS breakevens, surveys). The SRPC shifted up temporarily due to supply shocks, but expectations did not become unanchored. This allowed the Fed to raise rates without causing a deep recession (so far).

classifications.value: Changes in the natural rate of unemployment shift the long-run Phillips curve and also affect the short-run curve
characteristics: $u_n \uparrow$ (higher NAIRU): LRPC shifts right; SRPC shifts (the point where $\pi = \pi^e$ moves to higher $u$).

Causes: demographics (aging workforce lowers $u_n$?), labor market institutions (generous UI raises $u_n$), technology (skill mismatch raises $u_n$), globalization (outsourcing raises $u_n$?), hysteresis (recessions raise $u_n$).
Monetary policy cannot change $u_n$ (except possibly through hysteresis).
Structural policies (education, training, labor market reform) are needed to reduce $u_n$.
classifications.behavior:
The natural rate of unemployment (NAIRU) is not constant; it changes over time due to structural factors. A change in $u_n$ shifts the long-run Phillips curve (vertical line) and also affects the short-run curve because the reference point ($\pi = \pi^e$ at $u = u_n$) changes.

Decline in $u_n$ (1990s US): The natural rate fell from about 6% in the 1970s to about 4% by the late 1990s. Causes included: aging of the baby boom generation (older workers have lower unemployment), increased incarceration (removing young men from labor force), improved labor market matching (temp agencies, online job boards), and favorable supply shocks (IT productivity). The decline in $u_n$ allowed unemployment to fall to 4% without triggering inflation. Policymakers who did not recognize the decline would have tightened prematurely, causing an unnecessary recession.

Increase in $u_n$ (Europe 1980s): European unemployment rose to 10% and remained high for decades. The natural rate had increased due to labor market rigidities (high minimum wages, generous unemployment benefits, strong unions, employment protection laws). Structural reforms in the 2000s (e.g., Germany's Hartz reforms) reduced $u_n$ in some countries.

Hysteresis (2008 crisis): Prolonged high unemployment can permanently increase $u_n$ as workers lose skills and drop out of the labor force. The CBO's NAIRU estimate rose temporarily after the 2008 crisis (from 4.5% to 5.5%). This is an example of monetary policy affecting $u_n$ (though indirectly).

Post-COVID labor force exits: The pandemic caused many workers to retire early or leave the labor force. This may have increased $u_n$ (or at least reduced labor supply). The Fed's ability to lower unemployment without causing inflation may be reduced if $u_n$ has risen.

image.alt: Graph: LRPC shifting right (u_n increases) and left (u_n decreases); SRPC shifts accordingly.
image.caption: Figure 10.4.6: Changes in the natural rate of unemployment shift the long-run Phillips curve and also affect the short-run curve.

examples.explanation: The US natural rate fell from 6% to 4% due to demographics (aging workforce), increased incarceration, and improved labor market matching. Unemployment fell to 4% without inflation accelerating. The Fed did not tighten prematurely, recognizing the decline.

European unemployment remained at 10% for decades due to labor market rigidities (high minimum wages, generous benefits, strong unions). The natural rate had increased. Structural reforms (e.g., Germany's Hartz reforms) reduced the natural rate in the 2000s.

principles.principle: Supply Shocks Create a Painful Trade-Off for Central Banks: Fight Inflation or Accommodate
principles.statement: When an adverse supply shock hits (e.g., oil price spike, pandemic disruption), the SRPC shifts up. At the existing unemployment rate, inflation is higher. The central bank faces a dilemma: if it tightens monetary policy to fight inflation, unemployment will rise (possibly causing a recession); if it accommodates (keeps rates low), inflation may become entrenched. The 1970s oil shocks led to stagflation; the Fed initially accommodated, allowing inflation to rise. Volcker eventually tightened, causing a deep recession. The 2020-2021 supply shocks (pandemic) combined with demand stimulus caused inflation to surge; the Fed tightened aggressively, causing a slowdown but (so far) avoiding a deep recession. There is no free lunch: adverse supply shocks worsen the inflation-unemployment trade-off. Favorable supply shocks (productivity booms) improve the trade-off, allowing low unemployment without inflation.
principles.application: When faced with an adverse supply shock, central banks must decide how much to react. If the shock is temporary (e.g., supply chain disruption), the central bank may 'look through' it and keep policy accommodative. If the shock is persistent (e.g., energy price shift), the central bank must tighten to prevent inflation expectations from becoming unanchored. The Fed's 2022-2023 tightening was aggressive because the supply shocks proved persistent. The key is to anchor expectations: if the public believes the central bank will return inflation to target, the SRPC may not shift up as much.
principles.example: In 1973, the Fed accommodated the oil shock, keeping rates low. Inflation rose from 3% to 11%. By 1979, expectations were unanchored, and Volcker had to cause a deep recession to bring inflation down. In 2022, the Fed tightened aggressively despite the risk of recession, and by 2024 inflation had fallen from 9% to 3% with only a modest rise in unemployment. The difference: the Fed had credibility in 2022, which it lacked in the 1970s.

image.alt: Graph: SRPC shifts up (adverse supply shock). Central bank choices: accommodate (move to higher inflation) or fight (move to higher unemployment).
image.caption: Figure 10.4.7: Adverse supply shocks worsen the inflation-unemployment trade-off. Central banks face a dilemma: accommodate (higher inflation) or fight (higher unemployment).

principles.principle: Changes in the Natural Rate Require Structural Policies, Not Monetary Policy
principles.statement: The natural rate of unemployment is determined by real factors (demographics, labor market institutions, technology, globalization). Monetary policy cannot permanently change $u_n$ (except possibly through hysteresis). If the natural rate rises (e.g., due to labor market rigidities), the LRPC shifts right. The central bank cannot lower unemployment below the new natural rate without causing accelerating inflation. Structural policies (education, training, labor market reform, tax reform) are needed to reduce $u_n$. Conversely, if the natural rate falls (e.g., due to favorable demographics), the central bank can allow unemployment to fall to the new lower natural rate without causing inflation. Recognizing changes in $u_n$ is crucial for policy; tightening prematurely when $u_n$ has fallen would cause an unnecessary recession. The Fed learned this lesson in the 1990s when it did not tighten despite unemployment falling below the old NAIRU estimate.
principles.application: Central banks should not target unemployment directly; they should target inflation. If unemployment is low but inflation is stable, the natural rate may have fallen. The Fed should not tighten preemptively based on old NAIRU estimates. Structural policies (not monetary policy) are needed to reduce the natural rate. Governments should focus on education, training, labor market flexibility, and tax reform to lower $u_n$. Monetary policy can only smooth cyclical fluctuations around the natural rate.
principles.example: In the 1990s, the US natural rate fell from 6% to 4%. Unemployment fell to 4.5%, below the old NAIRU estimate of 5.5%. Many economists predicted inflation would rise. But inflation remained low. The Fed did not tighten prematurely, recognizing that the natural rate had fallen. This was a key policy success. In contrast, in the 1970s, the natural rate rose, but policymakers did not recognize it, leading to stagflation.

image.alt: Graph: LRPC shifts right (u_n increases). Monetary policy cannot lower u below un without causing inflation. Structural policies needed to reduce un.
image.caption: Figure 10.4.8: Changes in the natural rate require structural policies. Monetary policy cannot permanently reduce unemployment below the natural rate.

worked_examples.title: Analyzing the Effects of a Supply Shock on the Phillips Curve

image.alt: Table: Pre-shock SRPC: π = 2 - 0.5(u - 5). Oil shock: ν = +3. New SRPC: π = 5 - 0.5(u - 5). Calculations of inflation at different u.
image.caption: Figure 10.4.9: Worked example: analyzing how an adverse supply shock shifts the SRPC and affects inflation at a given unemployment rate.

worked_examples.problem:
The initial short-run Phillips curve is $\pi = 2 - 0.5(u - 5)$, with $u_n = 5\%$ and $\pi^e = 2\%$.

a) Calculate inflation when unemployment is 4%.
b) Calculate inflation when unemployment is 5%.
c) Calculate inflation when unemployment is 6%.

An adverse supply shock hits, adding $\nu = +3$ to the Phillips curve. The new SRPC is $\pi = 2 - 0.5(u - 5) + 3 = 5 - 0.5(u - 5)$.

d) Calculate inflation (after the shock) when unemployment is 4%.
e) Calculate inflation (after the shock) when unemployment is 5%.
f) If the central bank wants to keep inflation at 2% after the shock, what unemployment rate must it achieve?
g) Explain why the supply shock worsens the trade-off.

step_by_step.explanation:
$\pi = 5 - 0.5(4 - 5) = 5 - 0.5(-1) = 5 + 0.5 = 5.5\%$

Inflation is 3 percentage points higher than pre-shock (2.5% → 5.5%).

$\pi = 5 - 0.5(5 - 5) = 5 - 0 = 5\%$

Even at the natural rate, inflation is 5% (3 points higher than pre-shock).

step_by_step.step: Find unemployment to achieve 2% inflation after shock
step_by_step.explanation:
Set $\pi = 2$ and solve for $u$:
$2 = 5 - 0.5(u - 5)$
$0.5(u - 5) = 5 - 2 = 3$
$u - 5 = 3 / 0.5 = 6$
$u = 5 + 6 = 11\%$

The central bank must raise unemployment to 11% to bring inflation back to 2%.

Before the shock, reducing inflation from 2.5% to 2% required raising unemployment from 4% to 5% (a 1 point increase). After the shock, reducing inflation from 5.5% to 2% requires raising unemployment from 4% to 11% (a 7 point increase). The trade-off is much steeper: the same reduction in inflation requires a much larger increase in unemployment. This is because the SRPC has shifted up; to get back to the original inflation rate, the central bank must create a deep recession.

The supply shock worsens the inflation-unemployment trade-off. The central bank faces a dilemma: accept higher inflation (5.5% at u=4%) or cause a recession (u=11% to get back to 2% inflation). There is no good option. This is why supply shocks are so painful for policymakers.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) 2.5%. b) 2%. c) 1.5%. d) 5.5%. e) 5%. f) u = 11%. g) Supply shock shifts SRPC up; to reduce inflation back to original level requires a much larger increase in unemployment.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the Phillips curve framework to analyze how an adverse supply shock shifts the SRPC and worsens the inflation-unemployment trade-off.

worked_examples.title: Analyzing the Effects of a Decline in the Natural Rate

image.alt: Table: Old un=6%, new un=4%. SRPC: π = 2 - 0.5(u - un). Calculations of inflation at u=4% under both natural rates.
image.caption: Figure 10.4.10: Worked example: analyzing how a decline in the natural rate shifts the Phillips curve and affects inflation at a given unemployment rate.

worked_examples.problem:
The natural rate of unemployment falls from $u_n = 6\%$ to $u_n = 4\%$ due to demographic changes and labor market reforms. The Phillips curve is $\pi = 2 - 0.5(u - u_n)$ (with $\pi^e = 2\%$).

a) Calculate inflation when actual unemployment is 4% under the old natural rate ($u_n = 6\%$).
b) Calculate inflation when actual unemployment is 4% under the new natural rate ($u_n = 4\%$).
c) If the central bank targets 2% inflation, what unemployment rate should it aim for under the old natural rate? Under the new natural rate?
d) Explain why a decline in the natural rate is a 'free lunch' for policymakers.
e) What would happen if the central bank did not recognize the decline and kept policy based on the old natural rate?

step_by_step.step: Old natural rate: inflation at u=4%
step_by_step.explanation:
$\pi = 2 - 0.5(4 - 6) = 2 - 0.5(-2) = 2 + 1 = 3\%$

Under the old natural rate, unemployment of 4% (2 points below un) creates inflation of 3%.

step_by_step.step: New natural rate: inflation at u=4%
step_by_step.explanation:
$\pi = 2 - 0.5(4 - 4) = 2 - 0 = 2\%$

Under the new natural rate, unemployment of 4% (equal to un) creates inflation of 2% (stable).

Set $\pi = 2$ and solve for $u$:

Old natural rate: $2 = 2 - 0.5(u - 6)$ → $0 = -0.5(u - 6)$ → $u = 6\%$

New natural rate: $2 = 2 - 0.5(u - 4)$ → $0 = -0.5(u - 4)$ → $u = 4\%$

The central bank can target a lower unemployment rate (4% instead of 6%) while still achieving 2% inflation.

step_by_step.step: Why decline in natural rate is a 'free lunch'
step_by_step.explanation:
A decline in the natural rate allows the economy to operate at a lower unemployment rate without causing inflation to accelerate. This is a 'free lunch' for policymakers: they can have lower unemployment and stable inflation at the same time. There is no trade-off; both objectives improve. This is what happened in the US in the 1990s: unemployment fell to 4% without inflation rising. The Fed did not have to 'choose' between low unemployment and low inflation; both were achievable because the natural rate had fallen.

The free lunch is not free in the sense of policy cost; it is free because structural changes (demographics, technology, labor market reforms) have improved the economy's supply side. Monetary policy can take advantage of this by allowing unemployment to fall to the new lower natural rate.

step_by_step.step: Consequences of not recognizing the decline
step_by_step.explanation:
If the central bank did not recognize the decline in the natural rate and continued to target the old natural rate (6%), it would tighten policy when unemployment fell to 4-5%, believing that inflation would rise. This would cause an unnecessary recession. Unemployment would be forced back up to 6% even though the economy could sustain 4% unemployment with stable inflation. This is a policy error: the central bank would be 'fighting the last war.'

In the 1990s, many economists warned that unemployment below 5.5% would cause inflation. The Fed (under Greenspan) did not tighten prematurely, recognizing that the natural rate had fallen. This was a key policy success. The Fed learned from the experience and now uses a range of indicators (not just old NAIRU estimates) to guide policy.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) 3%. b) 2%. c) Old un: u=6%; new un: u=4%. d) Decline in un allows lower unemployment without higher inflation (both objectives improve). e) Tightening prematurely would cause unnecessary recession.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the Phillips curve framework to analyze how a decline in the natural rate improves the inflation-unemployment trade-off and the importance of recognizing structural changes.

process_flow.description: Track actual inflation (CPI, PCE) and unemployment (BLS) over time.
process_flow.mechanism: Plot (u, π) points to see if they are moving along a stable curve or shifting.

image.alt: Scatter plot: (u, π) points over time; identify movements vs. shifts.
image.caption: Figure 10.4.11: Step 1 - Observe inflation and unemployment data to see if the relationship is stable or shifting.

process_flow.process: Identify source of shift (expectations, supply shocks, or natural rate)
process_flow.description: Determine whether the shift is due to changes in π^e, supply shocks (ν), or changes in un.
process_flow.mechanism: Use auxiliary data: inflation expectations surveys, oil prices, productivity, labor force demographics.

image.alt: Decision tree: π^e rising? Supply shock? un changing?
image.caption: Figure 10.4.12: Step 2 - Identify the source of the shift using auxiliary data (expectations, oil prices, productivity, demographics).

process_flow.process: If supply shock, assess persistence (temporary vs. permanent)
process_flow.description: Determine whether the supply shock is likely to be temporary (e.g., supply chain disruption) or permanent (e.g., energy price shift).
process_flow.mechanism: If temporary, central bank may 'look through' it; if permanent, must adjust policy.

image.alt: Decision tree: Temporary supply shock → look through; Permanent → adjust policy.
image.caption: Figure 10.4.13: Step 3 - Assess the persistence of supply shocks to determine the appropriate policy response.

process_flow.process: If natural rate change, update estimates and adjust policy targets
process_flow.description: Revise estimates of the NAIRU and adjust the central bank's unemployment target.
process_flow.mechanism: Use models (CBO NAIRU, Kalman filter) to estimate un. If un has fallen, the central bank can allow lower unemployment without inflation.

image.alt: Diagram: Update NAIRU estimate → adjust policy (lower target u if un fell, higher if un rose).
image.caption: Figure 10.4.14: Step 4 - Update estimates of the natural rate and adjust policy targets accordingly.

process_flow.process: Design policy response to stabilize inflation
process_flow.description: Based on the source and persistence of the shift, determine the appropriate monetary policy response.
process_flow.mechanism: If shift is due to temporary supply shock → accommodate (allow inflation to rise temporarily). If shift is due to permanent supply shock or expectations → tighten to bring inflation back to target.

image.alt: Decision tree: Temporary supply shock → accommodate; Permanent shock or expectations → tighten; Natural rate change → adjust target.
image.caption: Figure 10.4.15: Step 5 - Design the appropriate monetary policy response based on the source and persistence of the shift.

image.alt: Master framework: Observe data → identify source (π^e, ν, un) → assess persistence (if supply shock) → update NAIRU (if natural rate change) → design policy response.
image.caption: Figure 10.4.16: Complete framework for analyzing shifts in the Phillips curve and designing policy responses.

summary: The Phillips curve shifts over time due to changes in expected inflation ($\pi^e$), supply shocks ($\nu$), and changes in the natural rate of unemployment ($u_n$). Adverse supply shocks (oil price increases, supply chain disruptions, pandemics) shift the SRPC up, causing stagflation (high inflation and high unemployment) and worsening the trade-off facing central banks. Favorable supply shocks (productivity booms, oil price decreases) shift the SRPC down, allowing low unemployment with low inflation. Changes in expected inflation shift the SRPC up (when $\pi^e$ rises) or down (when $\pi^e$ falls); credible central banks can anchor expectations to prevent shifts. Changes in the natural rate shift the long-run Phillips curve and also affect the SRPC; a decline in $u_n$ is a 'free lunch' that allows lower unemployment without inflation, while an increase in $u_n$ forces the central bank to accept higher unemployment for the same inflation target. Historical episodes illustrate these shifts: the 1970s oil crises (adverse supply shock, stagflation), the 1990s productivity boom (favorable supply shock, low unemployment with low inflation), the 2008 crisis (hysteresis, temporary increase in $u_n$), and the COVID-19 pandemic (adverse supply shock, inflation surge). Understanding shifts in the Phillips curve is essential for interpreting the inflation-unemployment relationship, forecasting inflation, and designing appropriate monetary policy responses.
